name: Present Simple
uses: Facts, general truths and permanent states.
Habits, routines and regular actions.
Timetables and schedules (future meaning).

examples.positive: I work every day. She speaks English fluently.
examples.negative: We don't live in London. He doesn't play tennis.
examples.question: Do you know him? Does the train leave at 12:00?

mistakes.correct: She speaks English. (Don't forget the -s for third person!)
He doesn't work here. (Auxiliary 'does' already takes the -s).

name: Past Simple
uses: Completed actions in the past at a specific time.
Past habits and states that are finished.

examples.positive: I visited Paris last year. They went to the cinema yesterday.
examples.negative: She didn't write the letter. We didn't see the movie.
examples.question: Did you sleep well? Where did they buy that car?

mistakes.correct: I didn't go to school. (Use infinitive after 'didn't').
She wrote a book. (Write is an irregular verb!).

name: Future Simple
uses: Instant, spontaneous decisions.
Predictions based on what we think or believe.
Promises, offers, and requests.

examples.positive: I will help you with your homework. The phone is ringing, I'll answer it.
examples.negative: We won't (will not) go to the party. He won't tell anyone.
examples.question: Will you marry me? What time will they arrive?

mistakes.correct: I will call you. (Don't use 'to' after modal verbs like 'will').

mistakes.incorrect: If it will rain, I will stay home.
mistakes.correct: If it rains, I will stay home. (In time and conditional clauses, use present simple for the future).

name: Present Continuous
uses: Actions happening right now, at the moment of speaking.
Temporary situations happening around now.
Fixed plans and arrangements in the near future.

examples.positive: I am working at the moment. They are playing football in the garden.
examples.negative: She isn't reading anything right now. We aren't watching TV.
examples.question: Are you listening to me? What is he doing over there?

mistakes.correct: I like this coffee. (Stative verbs like like, love, know, want, are rarely used in continuous tenses).
He is running. (Double the consonant for short vowels!).

name: Past Continuous
uses: An action that was in progress at a specific time in the past.
An ongoing action in the past interrupted by a shorter action (Past Simple).

examples.positive: I was studying when you called. They were driving home at 10 PM.
examples.negative: She wasn't paying attention. We weren't sleeping when it started raining.
examples.question: What were you doing yesterday at 8 PM? Was it snowing?

mistakes.correct: They were playing football. (Use 'were' for you, we, they).

name: Future Continuous
uses: An action that will be in progress at a specific point in the future.
Polite inquiries about someone's future plans.

examples.positive: This time tomorrow, I will be flying to New York. She will be sleeping by midnight.
examples.negative: I won't be working during the weekend. We won't be using that room.
examples.question: Will you be using your car tomorrow? What will they be doing?

mistakes.correct: I will be sleeping. (Always use base form 'be' after 'will').

name: Present Perfect Simple
formula: Subject + have/has + Verb-ED or Irregular Past Participle (V3)
uses: Actions in the past where the time is not mentioned and is not important.
Recent events with results in the present.
Life experiences (ever, never).
Actions that started in the past and continue in the present (with for/since).

examples.positive: I have lost my keys (I don't have them now). She has lived here since 2015.
examples.negative: We haven't seen that movie yet. He hasn't eaten lunch.
examples.question: Have you ever been to Rome? Has he finished his work?

mistakes.incorrect: I have bought a car yesterday.
mistakes.correct: I bought a car yesterday. (Do NOT use Present Perfect with specific past time expressions like 'yesterday', 'ago', 'in 2010').
She has lived here since 2015. (Use 'since' for a starting point, 'for' for a duration of time like 'for 5 years').

name: Past Perfect Simple
formula: Subject + had + Verb-ED or Irregular V3
uses: An action that happened before another action in the past.
Often used with 'by the time', 'before', 'after', 'when'.

examples.positive: When we arrived, the train had already left. She had eaten before she went out.
examples.negative: He hadn't studied for the exam, so he failed. We hadn't met before last week.
examples.question: Had you finished your homework before you went to bed? Had they left?

mistakes.incorrect: I had went to bed when he called.
mistakes.correct: I had gone to bed when he called. (Use past participle V3, not past simple V2).

name: Future Perfect Simple
formula: Subject + will have + Verb-ED or Irregular V3
uses: An action that will be completed by a specific point in the future.
Almost always used with 'by...' or 'by the time...'.

examples.positive: By next year, I will have graduated from university. She will have retired by 2030.
examples.negative: I won't have finished the report by tomorrow morning. We won't have eaten by 8 PM.
examples.question: Will you have arrived by 6 PM? Will they have completed the project by then?

mistakes.incorrect: By tomorrow I will have write it.
mistakes.correct: By tomorrow I will have written it. (Must use past participle V3 after 'will have').

name: Present Perfect Continuous
uses: Actions that started in the past, continue in the present, emphasizing the duration.
Actions that finished very recently and have a clear physical result in the present.

examples.positive: I have been working out for two hours (and I am exhausted). She has been learning English for years.
examples.negative: We haven't been sleeping well lately. He hasn't been studying much.
examples.question: How long have you been waiting here? Has it been raining?

mistakes.incorrect: I have been knowing him for years.
mistakes.correct: I have known him for years. (Do not use continuous forms with stative verbs like 'know').

name: Past Perfect Continuous
uses: An action that was in progress in the past before another action, emphasizing duration.
Shows cause and effect in the past.

examples.positive: He had been running for an hour before he collapsed. They had been waiting for two hours when the bus arrived.
examples.negative: We hadn't been expecting any good news. She hadn't been feeling well.
examples.question: How long had they been talking before they agreed? Had you been sleeping?

mistakes.incorrect: I was waiting for two hours when she arrived.
mistakes.correct: I had been waiting for two hours when she arrived. (To emphasize duration prior to another past action, use Past Perfect Continuous instead of Past Continuous).

name: Future Perfect Continuous
uses: An action that will be in progress in the future for a duration of time before a specific point.

examples.positive: In November, I will have been working here for 5 years. By 10 PM, she will have been sleeping for 3 hours.
examples.negative: I won't have been living here long enough to vote. We won't have been studying together for long.
examples.question: How long will you have been driving by the time we arrive? Will he have been training?

mistakes.incorrect: By next month, I will be working here for 5 years.
mistakes.correct: By next month, I will have been working here for 5 years. (Must use Future Perfect Continuous to describe continuous action before a future point).
